coredata.dc:title: A capillary electrophoretic method for separation and characterization of carbon dots and carbon dot-antibody bioconjugates
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yuanyuan
Remcho, Vincent T.
coredata.dc:description: Capillary electrophoresis (CE) coupled with UV absorbance detection has been applied as an analysis tool for carbon dots (C-dots), which were hydrothermally synthesized from precursors citric acid (CA) and ethylene diamine (EDA). An alkaline working buffer (100mM tris acetate, pH 8.4) was found to be optimal for rapid and reliable analysis of C-dots. A calibration curve was established over a broad concentration range (0.5–10mg/mL) with excellent linearity (R2=0.9989). The CE method was also applied to enhance understanding of and facilitate optimization of ensuing C-dot biolabeling reactions. The novel CE method was used to confirm and quantify production of C-dot labeled antibodies. The optimum concentration of antibody for use in the labeling reaction was determined.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Capillary electrophoresis
Carbon dot
Bioconjugation
Antibody

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Direct bioconjugation of antibodies to C-dots using glutaraldehyde
CE method for antibody C-dot conjugates
UV–visible and fluorescence spectra of C-dots
Effect of pH on CE analysis of C-dots
Reproducibility of CE analysis of C-dots
Calibration curve for CE analysis of C-dots
Optimization of antibody concentration in conjugation reactions via CE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–visible absorbance and fluorescence spectra of C-dots and quinine sulfate. Post-synthetic C-dots had characteristic absorption bands at 240nm and 350nm and exhibited a maximum emission wavelength at 440nm. Quinine sulfate had an absorption band at 350nm and maximum emission wavelength at 450nm.
TEM images of C-dots. Inset a: the size distribution of C-dots. Inset b and c: HR-TEM images of two single C-dots.

Electropherograms of aqueous solutions of C-dots (after desalting via dialysis) with run buffers of 100mM acetate or 100mM tris-acetate at different pHs: pH 4.36, pH 4.78, pH 5.21, pH 7.7 and pH 8.32. UV detection at 350nm. Electropherograms were offset for clarity and ease of comparison. * indicate the main peaks arising from the C-dot analyte.

Electropherograms for five consecutive runs of aqueous solutions of C-dots (10mg/mL) introduced at 10mbar pressure for 5s. Run buffer is 100mM tris-acetate, pH 8.4. Electropherograms were offset (only on y-axis) for clarity and ease of comparison. The migration time and peak areas of peaks labeled with asterisks (the main C-dot peak) were used for reproducibility evaluation.

a. Electropherograms for aqueous solutions of C-dots, with concentrations of 0.5, 2.5, 5 and 10mg/mL (bare silica capillary 38.5cm EL/47cm TL; ID 74µm; Injection 10mbar for 5s; Positive 20kV; working buffer tris-acetate (100mM, pH 8.4)). Electropherograms were offset at both x and y-axis for clarity and ease of comparison. b. The calibration curve between peak areas and concentrations of C-dots: peak area=10.714×concentration of C-dots(mg/mL)+0.4275; R2=0.9989 (n=5).

Electropherograms of components in C-dot and antibody conjugation reactions using glutaraldehyde (bare silica capillary 38.5cm EL/47cm TL; ID 74µm; Injection 10mbar for 5s; Positive 12kV; working buffer tris-acetate (100mM, pH 8.4)). From top down: electropherograms of glutaraldehyde, C-dots, unlabeled anti-AFP, C-dot labeled anti-AFP and 1:1 (v:v) ratio mixed unlabeled anti-AFP and C-dot labeled anti-AFP. Electropherograms were offset for clarity and ease of comparison.

Capillary electrophoretic optimization of anti-AFP labeling with C-dots. Various concentrations of anti-AFP were labeled with C-dots. Reaction mixtures were analyzed using CE. Inset: the percentage of C-dot-anti-AFP conjugate in the mixture (E%) was calculated and plotted against concentrations of anti-AFP added into the reactions.
Migration time and peak area statistics for the indicated (*) C-dot peaks in Fig. 4.

head.ce:title: A capillary electrophoretic method for separation and characterization of carbon dots and carbon dot-antibody bioconjugates

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Oregon State University, 153 Gilbert Hall, Corvallis, OR 97331, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Oregon State University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Capillary electrophoresis (CE) coupled with UV absorbance detection has been applied as an analysis tool for carbon dots (C-dots), which were hydrothermally synthesized from precursors citric acid (CA) and ethylene diamine (EDA). An alkaline working buffer (100mM tris acetate, pH 8.4) was found to be optimal for rapid and reliable analysis of C-dots. A calibration curve was established over a broad concentration range (0.5–10mg/mL) with excellent linearity (R2=0.9989). The CE method was also applied to enhance understanding of and facilitate optimization of ensuing C-dot biolabeling reactions. The novel CE method was used to confirm and quantify production of C-dot labeled antibodies. The optimum concentration of antibody for use in the labeling reaction was determined.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-dots), also called carbon nanodots and carbon nanoparticles, have recently been found to have great potential as alternatives to traditional heavy metal quantum dots and organic fluorophores, especially in biological applications, due to their favorable attributes such as high fluorescence, high photo- and chemical- stability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity and ease of preparation [1]. Among the wide variety of carbon nanomaterials under research, C-dots (derived from carbon and nitrogen precursors) have routinely exhibited higher fluorescence intensities per unit mass. For example, C-dots produced via bottom-up hydrothermal treatment of citric acid (CA) and ethylene diamine (EDA) achieved greater than 90% quantum yield [2]. An offsetting disadvantage, though, is that C-dots obtained from both top-down and bottom-up routes tend to exhibit a rather complex and heterogeneous character in terms of both size and surface functionality.

To fully explore the potential of C-dots as biocompatible fluorescent labels, C-dots are often linked to biomolecules with the help of bifunctional linkers such as EDC (1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride), homofunctional linkages such as glutaraldehyde, and electrostatic attraction. C-dots have been linked to biorecognition units such as antibodies [3] and nucleic acids (aptamers) [4], and have been successfully applied to immunoassays [3a], DNA hybridization and cell imaging [5]. In these applications, C-dot bioconjugation plays a vital role in determining sensitivity. It is clear that all of these applications would benefit from the efficient separation of conjugates from free C-dots and biorecognition units (proteins and DNAs).

As an open tubular separation technique, capillary electrophoresis (CE) offers inherent advantages over packed column chromatography and slab gel electrophoresis for nanomaterial separation and characterization. CE has been successfully applied to separation and characterization of a series of nanoparticles [6], including QDs and QD conjugates with biomolecules [7]. Carbon nanomaterials, such as chemically converted grapheme [8], nanodiamonds [9] and carbonaceous soot [10], have also been analyzed by CE. Hu et al. [11] applied CE to a study of how synthetic conditions, such as mole ratios of NH2/COOH in precursors and durations of microwave heating time, influenced the surface-functionality of the resulting C-dot species, aiming at finding the optimum CE conditions for separation of positively, neutral and negatively charged C-dots.

In this paper, we describe a novel, simple, rapid CE method for: (1) quantitation of C-dots, and (2) analysis of a C-dot-antibody bioconjugate, thus providing a method for optimization of conditions for acquiring the desire6d C-dot bioconjugate. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work focused on applying CE as a diagnostic tool for C-dot labeling reactions.

Anhydrous citric acid (enzyme grade, 99.5%) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA). Ethylenediamine (SigmaUltra) and Glutaraldehyde (Grade 1, 50% aqueous solution) were purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, MO). Mouse derived anti-human antibody for alpha-fetoprotein was purchased from BiosPacific (Emeryville, CA). Biotech CE Tubing (MWCO 100–500Da; 100kDa) for dialysis was purchased from Spectrum Labs (Rancho Dominguez, CA). Centrifugal ultrafiltration units were purchased from Pall Corporation (Port Washington, NY) and Millipore (Billerica, MA). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and ultrapure water (Millipore, Billerica, MA) was used for all aqueous solutions. Samples and buffers for use with the CE system were filtered through 0.2µm membranes.

A Hewlett Packard 3D capillary electrophoresis system, equipped with a diode array UV–Vis detector, was used for CE analysis. Bare silica capillaries were purchased from Polymicro Technologies (Phoenix, AZ). A scanning transmission electron microscopy (TEM/STEM) system (Model TITAN 80-200, FEI, Hillsboro, OR) was used for morphology characterization of C-dots. A Hewlett–Packard Model 8453 UV–vis absorbance spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA) and a SpectraMax Gemini XS microplate fluorimeter (Molecular Devices, Sunnyvale, CA) were used for optical characterization and measurements.

C-dots were synthesized from citric acid (CA) and ethylene diamine (EDA) (molar ratio CA:EDA=1:4) using a hydrothermal reaction described previously [3a]. The crude carbon dot solution (10mL) was dialyzed with ultrapure water (2L) for 2 days using Biotech CE tubing (MWCO 100–500Da). The dialysate was monitored by fluorescence spectrometer and the dialysis process was stopped when the fluorescence of dialysate was low and stable. Aqueous C-dot solutions were stored under dark conditions at room temperature. Samples of C-dots were dried and weighed to produce solutions of various concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct bioconjugation of antibodies to C-dots using glutaraldehyde
ce:section.ce:para: Purified C-dot stock solution (3040µL, 10mg/mL) was mixed with diluted glutaraldehyde (200µL, 5% (w/v)), and stirred for 1h prior to adding the antibody (anti-AFP in 100mM PBS) to reaction flask. The conjugation reaction was kept stirring at room temperature overnight, then transferred to an ice bath. 800µL of freshly prepared 20mg/mL sodium borohydride was then added dropwise to the reaction, with stirring, in an ice bath. The reduction reaction was maintained at 4°C for two days before the solutions were directly analyzed by CE, unless otherwise stated. C-dot labeled anti-AFP was isolated by dialysis against 1×PBS for 2 days using Biotech CE tubing with MWCO 100kDa to remove excess unlabeled C-dots and salts, with several buffer changes. Alternatively, centrifugal ultrafiltration units (MWCO 50kDa) were used for this purpose, with multi-fold washing using 1×PBS buffer.

A bare silica capillary with total length of 47cm, effective length of 38.5cm, and inner diameter of 74µm was used to determine the concentrations of C-dots. Each new capillary was etched with 1M NaOH and then with 0.1M NaOH each for 30min. The capillary was then flushed with water and working buffer for 30min each. Aqueous C-dot samples were introduced at a pressure of 10mbar for 5s. Positive voltage of 20kV was applied for electrophoretic separation of the C-dots. UV absorbance at 240nm and 350nm were monitored. Between-run conditioning was carried out by flushing for 1min with 1M NaOH, 1min with water and 2min with working buffer. The working buffer was 100mM tris-acetate, pH 8.4. Five C-dot samples (0.5, 2.5, 5, 10 and 15mg/mL) were analyzed five times each using this CE method, and peak areas were plotted against concentrations to obtain a calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CE method for antibody C-dot conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: The same capillary as above was used for separation and characterization of C-dot labeled antibodies. The antibody sample, labeled with C-dots, was introduced using a pressure of 10mbar for 5s. Positive voltage of 12kV was applied for separation. UV absorbance at 280nm was monitored for glutaraldehyde, C-dot labeled anti-AFP and unlabeled anti-AFP; 240nm and 350nm were monitored for unlabeled C-dots. Between-run conditioning was carried out by flushing the capillary for 3min with 1M NaOH, 3min with water and 6min with working buffer. The working buffer was 100mM tris-acetate, pH 8.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–visible and fluorescence spectra of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Unmodified C-dots exhibited absorption bands at 240 and 350nm, while the fluorescence emission scanning spectra showed a maximum emission at 440nm when excitation at 350nm ( Fig. 1). The 240nm absorption band is usually associated with the π→π* transition of the sp2 carbon in C-dots, while the 350nm absorption band is usually associated with the n→π* transition of C=O carbonyl groups of C-dots. The absorption at both 240nm and 350nm made a UV–visible diode array detector equipped CE system suitable for C-dot analysis.

The morphology of as-prepared C-dots was analyzed by TEM ( Fig. 2). The diameter distribution of the C-dots fell in the range of 2–10nm, with a majority in the 4–6nm range. The average diameter was 6±2nm (n=23; Fig. 2, inset a). The HR-TEM images revealed some crystallinity, with the spacing measured to be 0.26nm, which is in keeping with the diffraction planes of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2, insets b and c). These results were in close agreement with C-dot morphology data reported by others for C-dots produced in similar fashion to those produced for this study [12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on CE analysis of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The initial ATR-FTIR characterization work showed that, as-synthesized, C-dots have vibrational stretches indicating O–H, N–H, and C=O bonds, as well as bending of N–H bonds, in IR spectra [3a]. The abundance of –OH, –C=O, –COOH and –NH2 moieties accounted for the aqueous suspendability and the polymerization ability of C-dots with various inorganic, organic, or biologically active substances [13]. No noticeable precipitation was observed for C-dot aqueous suspension after storage at room temperature for several months. This suspension stability makes CE an ideal tool for their analysis. According to studies by Hu et al. [11], an acidic running buffer (pH 3.6) was optimal in providing effective separation of positive and neutral C-dots, but yielded an average run time of more than 30min. Aimed at obtaining a fast quantitation method for C-dots and providing some guidance on bioconjugation reactions, we used 100mM acetate buffers (pH 4.36, pH 4.78 and pH 5.21) and 100mM tris-acetate buffers (pH 7.7 and pH 8.32) as working buffers for C-dot separation and quantitation. The migration times of C-dots decreased with increasing pH from 4.36 to 8.32, as the bulk electroosmotic flow increases accordingly ( Fig. 3). Since the bulk electroosmotic flow increased at a rate greater than the electrophoretic mobility of the C-dots, the bulk flow swept the C-dots more rapidly toward the cathode without adversely influencing C-dot electrophoretic zone profiles. In an alkaline run buffer (tris-acetate, pH 8.32) the analysis time for C-dots was significantly reduced (from 80min to under 20min, Fig. 3), which was different from results obtained by Hu et al. [11] using 30mM alkaline phosphate buffers, where only broad bands of unresolved peaks were observed. Significantly high currents were observed from our trials using the Hu et al. phosphate buffers in our CE system, which suggests that the high conductivity of this phosphate buffer may have led to appreciable Joule heating in this CE analysis. Also, the interactions of different buffer components with both the C-dots and the capillary wall may have influenced their surface properties, leading to different electrophoretic mobilities and electroosmotic flows, therefore, resulting in zone spreading and loss of resolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility of CE analysis of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Optimization of CE conditions led to the use of bare silica capillaries with total length (TL) of 47cm, effective length (EL) of 38.5cm, and inner diameter (ID) of 74µm for all subsequent analyses of C-dots. Aqueous C-dot samples were introduced at a pressure of 10mbar for 5s. Positive voltage of 20kV was applied to generate a cathodic flow. UV absorbance at 240nm and 350nm were monitored. A tris-acetate (100mM, pH 8.4) working buffer was used. As shown in Fig. 4, five consecutive analyses of aqueous C-dots (10mg/mL) were carried out to assess the reproducibility of the CE method. Each run required less than 10min. The electropherograms for the C-dots, compared to that of a neutral marker, dimethylformamide (2.77min), indicated that the main peaks for the C-dots at 5.88min (Fig. 4, peak with asterisks) were negatively charged and accounted for the majority of C-dot population. The relative standard deviations (RSD %) of migration times and peak areas for these predominant peaks were below 3%, indicating excellent reproducibility for this novel CE method ( Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve for CE analysis of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Samples of C-dot stock solutions (after dialysis purification) were filtered through 0.22µm membrane disks and dried, and stock solution concentration was then determined by weighing. C-dot stock solutions were diluted with water to yield samples of various concentrations (0.5, 2.5, 5 and 10mg/mL), which were then analyzed using the fast CE method established above. Each sample was analyzed 5 times; run time in each instance was less than 10min. Since the absorbance of C-dots at 240nm was much higher than that at 350nm, the peak areas for the principal component (peaks with asterisks, Fig. 5a) at 240nm were plotted against their sample concentrations. A linear regression calibration curve, with a slope of 10.714 and intercept of 0.4275, was obtained, with an R-squared value of 0.9989 (Fig. 5b). The linear range was from 0.5 to 10mg/mL. A C-dot sample of 15mg/mL was also analyzed, though its peak area fell outside of the linear range. This calibration curve was then used to quickly determine concentrations of C-dots for subsequent antibody biolabeling reactions.

C-dots were directly applied as highly fluorescent biolabels to antibodies. The amine functional groups of the C-dots were linked with the bioactive proteins through a homofunctional crosslinker, glutaraldehyde. The C-dot labeled antibodies were successfully applied to wellplate based sandwich immunoassays for alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) in our previous work [3a]. CE provides a convenient tool not just for rapid quantitation of C-dots, but also for characterization of the bioconjugate products. After glutaraldehyde crosslinking, dialysis was carried out to remove excess glutaraldehyde. The C-dot labeled anti-AFP was then analyzed by CE using the method described here.

C-dot labeled anti-AFP (CD-anti-AFP, red line Fig. 6) exhibited one main peak at 7.1min. This migration time was in-between that of anti-AFP (6.1min, green line Fig. 6) and unlabeled C-dots (10.6min, orange line Fig. 6). The conjugated CD-anti-AFP carried a more substantial negative charge than the unlabeled anti-AFP arising from the attached, negatively charged C-dots. Citric-acid-derived C-dots have the demonstrated properties of being small in size (nominally 5nm [12]), low in mass (typically only a few kDa [14]) and not separable from solution via centrifugation. Thus, dialysis has been the most widely applied method for purification of C-dots. Dialysis tubing having a low molecular weight cutoff (MWCO), for instance 100Da [12f], 100–500Da [12b], 1kDa [15], and 3.5–5kDa [12d], were therefore used in our C-dot and C-Dot antibody conjugate studies. The increased negative charge on the conjugate was possibly the main causative factor in the shift of migration to a longer time in this case, because the attached C-dots, being small relative to the conjugate, made the size difference between CD-anti-AFP and anti-AFP a very minor influencing factor in determining migration rate. However, compared to the un-functionalized C-dots, CD-anti-AFP conjugates have a much larger size. The apparent electrophoretic mobility is dependent on both the bulk EOF and the electrophoretic mobility of the analyte. The electrophoretic mobility of the analyte is dependent on charge-to-size ratio, while the direction of migration is dependent on the sign of the charge. Therefore, bulky, negatively-charged species (CD-anti-AFP) migrate faster than small, negatively-charged species (CD) in the cathodic flow. CD-anti-AFP had a shorter migration time than that of the C-dots.

To demonstrate that C-dot labeled anti-AFP was a new product produced from the conjugation reaction, this conjugate CD-anti-AFP was mixed with unlabeled anti-AFP in a 1:1 (v:v) ratio. Two peaks showed up in the electropherogram (blue line Fig. 6) for this mixture: a peak at 6.2min matched that of anti-AFP; the other peak at 7.3min matched that of CD-anti-AFP. Minimal unreacted glutaraldehyde remained in the conjugate sample after ultra-filtration purification, as evidenced by comparing the electropherogram for the CD-anti-AFP with that for glutaraldehyde (5.3min, purple line Fig. 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of antibody concentration in conjugation reactions via CE
ce:section.ce:para:
The nanoliter sample size required by CE makes it a convenient tool for bioconjugation optimization. CE was applied to optimization of C-dot labeling of antibodies. The optimum concentration of antibody in the conjugation reaction was evaluated. Reaction mixtures (before purification) were directly analyzed by CE. The percentage of C-dot-anti-AFP conjugate in the mixture (E%) was approximately calculated using the following formula [7]:
(1)
E % =
A
c o n j
t
c o n j
A
c o n j
t
c o n j
+
A
f r e e
t
f r e e
× 100
where
A
conj
and
A
free
expressed the peak areas of the C-dot-anti-AFP conjugate and the free C-dots, and
t
conj
and
t
free
were the migration times of the C-dot-anti-AFP conjugate and the free C-dots respectively. The absorption channel at 214nm was used in this equation adopted from Huang et al. [7], which is tenable so long as the absorptivity of the C-dot-anti-AFP conjugate and free C-dots at 214nm have no significant difference. The percentage of C-dot-anti-AFP conjugate increased rapidly as more anti-AFP was added into the reaction mixture (from 0.03 to 0.14mg/mL, Fig. 7). The growth trend slowed down when more than 0.14mg/mL anti-AFP was in the reaction (Fig. 7, inset). Therefore, in order to both maintain high labeling efficiency and preserve expensive antibody reagent, 0.14mg/mL of anti-AFP in the C-dot conjugation reaction was determined to be the optimum concentration.

In summary, a rapid CE method has been developed for C-dot separation and quantitation. As-synthesized, C-dots, obtained by hydrothermal treatment of CA and EDA, exhibit high quantum yield. The composition and concentration of C-dots in the final crude or purified product is difficult to determine through UV absorption and fluorescence measurement, where an average property of C-dot species is obtained and self-quenching effects are also an issue. Drying and weighing, on the other hand, is both time-consuming and reagent-consuming. In this paper, CE was applied as an alternative means to determine both the composition and the quantity of C-dots. By increasing the pH of running buffer, the migration times of C-dots decreased as the electroosmotic flow increased. With our CE method using basic tris-acetate working buffer, the analysis of each sample can be achieved in under 10min. Consecutive analyses of C-dot samples under 10mg/mL demonstrated the great reliability of this approach. Also, a calibration curve was established between peak areas and concentrations of C-dots with good linearity, which was then used for C-dot quantification in its conjugation reactions. The calibration curve established here is specific to the approach taken here for sythesis, and would require adjustment for C-dots synthesized using alternative methods.

CE was also applied to separate and characterize C-dot labeling of antibodies. Glutaraldehyde, in this case, was used as the crosslinker between C-dots and protein. With our CE method, all of the components (glutaraldehyde, unlabeled antibody, C-dot, C-dot labeled antibody) can be well separated. Also, a new product, which migrated between the unlabeled antibody and C-dots in the electropherogram, was shown to be C-dot labeled antibodies. This CE method was then applied to optimize the amount of antibody added into the conjugation reaction. The C-dot labeled anti-AFP was then applied to an immunosorbent assay for AFP [3a]. The CE system employed in these studies is an early production unit, which may have caused unstable performance including variable peak shapes and compromised reproducibility. However, the CE approach as discussed here has great potential to be applied as a characterization tool for other C-dot biomolecule labeling reactions. While we were resolution limited in these experiments, in future experiments we hope to more closely explore particle size distribution, extent of C-dot conjugation of the antibody, and the structure of each component of the C-dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to Dr. Peng Wei for his guidance in conjugation reactions and editorial assistance; and Emily A. Schumacher for her volunteer lab assistance.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives

Strongly green-photoluminescent graphene quantum dots for bioimaging applications
Facile synthesis of water-soluble, highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a robust biological label for stem cells
Capillary electrophoresis, mass spectrometry, and UV–visible absorption studies on electrolyte-induced fractionation of gold nanoclusters
Studying the size/shape separation and optical properties of silver nanoparticles by capillary electrophoresis
Characterization of quantum dot bioconjugates by capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescent detection
Capillary zone electrophoresis of graphene oxide and chemically converted graphene
Separation and characterisation of detonation nanodiamond by capillary zone electrophoresis
Influence of buffer composition on the capillary electrophoretic separation of carbon nanoparticles
Capillary electrophoretic study of amine/carboxylic acid-functionalized carbon nanodots
Synthesis of hydrophobic photoluminescent carbon nanodots by using l-tyrosine and citric acid through a thermal oxidation route
Control of photoluminescence of carbon nanodots via surface functionalization using para-substituted anilines

Unravelling the multiple emissive states in citric-acid-derived carbon dots

Preparation and application of fluorescent carbon dots
Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions
